verbatim_text: And finally transformers can implement the associative algorithm. What this does is you can combine groups of two inputs in the first layer, groups of four in the next layer, groups of eight in the following layer, and subsequently exponentially longer groups of inputs. And you can get this sort of like tree-like hierarchical algorithm like this. Okay, so now that we've established several hypothesized algorithms, we're ready to ask what algorithms do transformers actually implement. And to answer this question, we're going to revisit the two techniques from earlier, probing and activation patching. And starting from probing, we're specifically going to do a variant where we're going to do the same thing. We're specifically going to do a variant where we try to probe out from each layer the current state at each time step. So for example, we're going to train an embedding probe to extract all the states from the embedding layer. Next we're going to train a layer zero probe to extract all the states from the layer zero representation. And so on we're going to go down the layers like this. And specifically this is going to tell us for each state at a time step t, at which layer is it first computed. Okay, so we get a kind of heat map that looks something like this. So yellow means the probe is 100% accurate, blue means the probe is 0% accurate. So the critical takeaway is we can kind of draw the boundary line between the yellow and blue.
